title: Dog Constant Head Shaking and Whining: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Find out why your dog is shaking their head and whining. Learn about ear infections, hematomas, and when you need to see a vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Persistent head shaking and whining indicate ear pain typically caused by infections or foreign objects, requiring a veterinary exam within 24 hours to avoid complications like hematomas.

What are the common causes for a dog to shake their head and whine?

  • Ear infection (otitis externa): A very common cause of localized inflammation and pain.
  • Foreign objects: Items like grass seeds can become lodged deep within the ear canal.
  • Ear mites: Parasitic infestations that cause intense irritation and distress.
  • Acute pain: Whining indicates the discomfort has moved beyond an itch to significant distress.

When is my dog's head shaking considered a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Medium. While not immediately life-threatening, you should seek care within 24 hours.
  • Risk of Complications: Untreated shaking can cause aural hematomas (blood-filled ear flaps) or permanent middle ear damage.
  • Immediate Care Signs: Seek emergency help if you notice a persistent head tilt, loss of balance, or flickering eye movements.

How does sharing a photo of my dog's ear help with triage?

  • Visual Assessment: A clear photo allows a professional to examine the inner ear flap and the opening of the ear canal remotely.
  • Identifying Discharge: Images help identify inflammation, crusting, or the color of discharge (black, waxy, or purulent).
  • Determining Treatment: Visual evidence helps distinguish between a surface infection and an emergency foreign body that may require sedation.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, constant head shaking and whining can be associated with otitis, often accompanied by pruritus. When evaluating a dog exhibiting these signs, determining the severity of pain via gentle palpation is crucial; sedation may be necessary for further diagnostics if discomfort is significant. Underlying neurologic disorders should also be considered, particularly sensory neuropathies, cauda equina syndrome, pseudorabies, neoplasia of peripheral nerves, or syringomyelia/Chiari-like malformation, although these typically manifest with pruritus, pain, or self-mutilation. Other causes include respiratory tract diseases and parasites, ear and eye disease, GI disorders, pain, trauma, and nasal foreign bodies.

Chapter: Neurology, Infectious Disease, Dermatology, Respiratory

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 974)

faqs.question: Should I try to clean my dog's ears at home before the vet visit?
faqs.answer: No, it is best to avoid putting any liquids or cleaners into the ear until a vet has confirmed the eardrum is intact. If the eardrum is ruptured, many common cleaners can cause permanent deafness.

faqs.question: What is an aural hematoma?
faqs.answer: An aural hematoma occurs when violent head shaking causes a blood vessel inside the ear flap to burst, filling the ear leather with blood and creating a painful, pillow-like swelling.

faqs.question: Can allergies cause constant head shaking?
faqs.answer: Yes, chronic allergies are a leading cause of recurring ear infections in dogs, which leads to the inflammation and pain that causes head shaking.
